dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for a priority 2 abdominal pain call. Later changed to priority 1 due to call type being unconscious/fainting/near-fainting.
Hi, I'm Mark, an EMT with the emergency team. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having severe lower abdominal pain that's 10 out of 10 since this morning.
Can you tell me your name and where you are right now?
My name is Lisa, and I'm in my bedroom in Richmond.
What day and time do you think it is?
I'm not sure about the time, but it's Friday the 13th.
Lisa, we want to check your blood pressure now.
Ma'am, your current blood pressure is 129 over 70 mmHg.
We're checking your pulse now. It's 45 beats per minute.
We're also measuring your respirations, Lisa. Your respiratory rate is 22 breaths per minute.
We'll check your blood sugar now, you're going to feel a small pinch.
Your blood sugar is 111 mg/dL, which is within normal range.
And your oxygen saturation is at 100 percent.
Your airway is patent and there's no sign of obstruction.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: Your breathing is normal in rate and depth with no signs of distress.

dialogue.micro_intent: check circulation - Pulse rate and quality
dialogue.utterance: Your pulse is strong but unusually slow, which could be concerning.
Your skin is pale and cool with some diaphoresis.
Your skin is cool, diaphoretic, and pale. No evidence of rash or redness.
No rashes, bruises, or open wounds on your skin.
On a scale of 1 to 10, how bad is the abdominal pain?
It's a 10, the worst I've ever felt.
Have you been vomiting? Can you tell me how many times?
I've vomited several times and feel very nauseous.
Any prior medical conditions or surgeries?
I have moderate asthma and I still have my appendix.
Are you currently taking medications or have prescriptions?
I can't recall, but I use medication as needed.
When was the last time you ate or drank something?
I haven't eaten, just some water.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Can you describe when and how the pain started this morning?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Pain started when I woke up and has been constant.
Lisa, we're going to assess your abdomen. Can you tell us what you feel?
Uh, okay, go ahead.
Your abdomen is soft but very tender in the lower quadrants.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Mental status
dialogue.utterance: Are you still experiencing chest pain or shortness of breath?

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Mental status
dialogue.utterance: No, just abdominal pain.
Your asthma is noted; have you used an inhaler recently?
No, because my asthma isn't acting up right now.
Are you pregnant or could be?
No, I'm not pregnant.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: When was the last treatment you had for similar pain?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Once before. They gave me something that "froze my stomach" and stopped the pain.
We are following protocol 3-2 for medical abdominal pain.

dialogue.micro_intent: take procedures - iv start - extremity vein (arm or leg)
dialogue.utterance: We're going to start an IV in your arm now.

dialogue.micro_intent: take procedures - iv start - extremity vein (arm or leg)
dialogue.utterance: IV has been placed in your right arm.

dialogue.micro_intent: take procedures - cv - ecg - 4 lead monitoring
dialogue.utterance: We'll attach heart monitors now.

dialogue.micro_intent: take procedures - cv - ecg - 12 lead obtained
dialogue.utterance: Performing a 12-lead ECG for cardiac assessment.

dialogue.micro_intent: take procedures - cv - ecg - 4 lead monitoring
dialogue.utterance: EKG results are normal now beyond slight bradycardia with a first-degree block.
Administering 500 mL of normal saline for hydration support.
We will give you 4 mg Zofran intravenously to help with the nausea.
Rechecking your pulse - it is now 50 beats per minute.
Your blood pressure is 116 over 77, with a slight drop.
Rechecking your blood sugar - remains stable at 111 mg/dL.
Your oxygen remains at 100 percent.
Your blood pressure decreased further to 110 over 67 - mild hypotension.
You remain not hypoglycemic with glucose at 111 mg/dL.
Your body temperature is 96.8°F - a bit cool, but not critically hypothermic.
Continuous heart and vital sign monitoring remains active.
Your IV remains patent, and your EKG readings are consistent.
Lisa, have you had any bowel movements or loose stools today?
Yes, I had some brief diarrhea around noon.
We're transporting you to VCU for evaluation of your abdominal pain and heart findings.
You'll need to assist with standing a short distance so we can place you on the stretcher.
You are now secure on the stretcher with a 5-point harness.
Apollo, we're en route to VCU ED with Lisa, a 30-year-old female.
Primary complaint is severe abdominal pain with associated first-degree heart block.
She has moderate asthma, no allergies, and is currently experiencing her menstrual cycle.
Given 500mL NS IV and 4mg Zofran. Mild improvement observed with stable vitals in-transit.
Her oxygen saturation dropped to 98 before, but remained otherwise stable.
She remains alert with a GCS of 15 and will be transferred by 3-person lift to the trauma bay.
Transportation will be direct to the trauma bay at VCU for an immediate ER assessment.
Continuous heart and vital sign monitoring will occur during transport per protocol.
Re-check of vital signs will ensure we have updated information for the hospital.
Let me check your pain level again - how is it now?
Still 10 out of 10, not improving but I'm managing the pain.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness - signs and symptoms
dialogue.utterance: Any new or worsening symptoms since we've started assessing?

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness - signs and symptoms
dialogue.utterance: No new symptoms, only persistent abdominal pain and occasional nausea.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness - medications
dialogue.utterance: Any over-the-counter medications or supplements in use lately?

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness - medications
dialogue.utterance: No new meds recently used.
Change in pain through the day or certain activities?
No, it's constant - same since waking up.
Your skin remains cool with no significant change.
No rashes, bites or abnormalities observed on your skin.
Any known drug allergies?
No known drug allergies.
The medicine before helped - could you remember where or when it was used?
I think it was at VCU. They gave something to "freeze my stomach."
Continuous EKG monitoring will continue during transport.
You will stay in your current position during the remainder of the ride.
Any ER treatment or clinic visits for similar pain in the past?
Only once - when I got that medicine.
We'll make sure to inform the hospital about that prior treatment.
Let me make a small adjustment to your stretcher for comfort.
Heart rate remains consistent during this check.
All readings are consistent and being transferred live to the hospital.
All systems appear ready. Your current vitals and conditions are consistent.
We will monitor your IV and EKG for the remaining time.
Ambulance is ready and available for any other responding calls.
Your IV and monitoring remain intact and secure.
No deviation from the protocol has been observed so far.
Patient has a medical history of abdominal pain and a current heart block.
VCU Emergency Department remains the confirmed destination.
